guest_role: Founder and Managing Partner of Sendana Capital (fund of funds investing in seed funds)
assertiveness_why: Harry repeatedly cites outside voices' contrary opinions (a panelist calling seed money 'dumb', Chris Douglas on LP/GP convergence, Dave McClure's portfolio-spread thesis) and asks Kim to respond/defend, going beyond pure reception.
question_style: Structured, well-researched questions that often import a third party's counter-argument and ask the guest to rebut it directly; ends with a quickfire round. Harry doesn't interrupt, but he does plant others' theses against the guest's positions.
tone: Warm, complimentary and matey ('such a pleasure', 'huge hand to Michael'), enthusiastic energy, heavy sponsor reads (Design Crowd) bookending the episode.
depth: Reasonably technical on fund mechanics — ownership percentages, dilution math, portfolio construction, seed round composition, mortality rates — showing Harry tracks VC economics specifics rather than staying purely narrative.
harry_quotes: Do you agree that even if it is dumb money, is that a bad thing?
So how do you respond to that, then? I'm intrigued.
What do you make of this kind of narrowing of the VC funding environment this year?
learnings: Kim argues seed funds succeed by leading rounds and writing the largest checks to secure 10-15% ownership, since small ownership stakes require billion-dollar exits to be meaningful.
He disagrees with a 'spray and pray' portfolio philosophy, contending concentrated ownership generates better fund returns than a large number of tiny positions.
LPs investing directly in startups face adverse selection risk, since the best deals rarely trickle down to LPs before top-tier VCs claim allocation.
Kim's actual seed-portfolio mortality rate has run under 10%, well below the 80% figure often assumed, though he expects it to normalize toward 25-30%.
He evaluates seed-fund GPs on three filters: ecosystem strength, portfolio construction discipline, and intangible factors like network and long-term platform ambition.
repeat_guest_callback: I had Jeff on the show a while ago. Jeff and Andy McLaughlin on the show a while ago.
notable: Harry name-drops having interviewed Dave McClure the night before at a fireside chat and floats hosting a future roundtable between Kim and McClure to hash out their portfolio-construction disagreement live.
